---
# ============================================================
# LECTURE TEMPLATE — copy this file when starting a new lecture.
# Filename convention: NN-short-slug.qmd  (e.g., 02-history-clinical-relationship.qmd)
# Files prefixed with "_" are skipped by Quarto, so this template
# itself is not rendered.
# ============================================================
title: "Lecture N — [Lecture Title]"
subtitle: "Part A: [Subtitle] · Part B: [Subtitle]"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  revealjs:
    output-file: NN-slug-slides.html
---

# Part A — [Subtitle]

## Opening: A Case to Sit With

Open with a brief, concrete scenario — one paragraph or a short quote — that frames the lecture's central tension. This is the hook, not the lesson.

## What We're Doing Today

::: {.learning-outcomes}
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

- [Outcome 1: action verb + specific content + condition if relevant]
- [Outcome 2]
- [Outcome 3]
:::

## [Section 1 Title]

Body text. Introduce a [key term]{.key-term} the first time it appears so the styling cues students that it's a concept they should be able to define.

In-text citations follow Chicago author-date and use the standard Pandoc syntax: @beauchamp2019 argued that the four principles function as *prima facie* duties. A parenthetical reference looks like this [@emanuel1992]. You can cite multiple works at once [@beauchamp2019; @faden1986].

::: {.context-box}
**[Concept students may not know]**

Brief background — one to three short paragraphs — for any concept that wouldn't be familiar to a 12th grader. Keep this scaffolding tight. Examples: what an RCT is, what germline vs. somatic means, how brain death is determined.
:::

## [Section 2 Title]

When a diagram clarifies the structure of an argument or relationship, prefer Mermaid over a static image — it stays editable.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Patient autonomy] --> D{Conflict?}
  B[Clinical beneficence] --> D
  D -->|No| E[Proceed with care plan]
  D -->|Yes| F[Ethics consultation]
```

Use Graphviz when the layout requires it (decision trees, larger graphs):

```{dot}
digraph G {
  rankdir=LR;
  node [shape=box, style=rounded];
  "Refuses treatment" -> "Has capacity?";
  "Has capacity?" -> "Honor refusal" [label="yes"];
  "Has capacity?" -> "Surrogate decides" [label="no"];
}
```

When you need a photograph or historical image, download from Wikimedia Commons (CC-licensed) into `images/` and attribute with a Quarto figure caption. Example:

![Hippocrates of Kos. Engraving by Peter Paul Rubens, 1638. *Wikimedia Commons*, public domain.](../images/hippocrates.jpg){#fig-hippocrates width="40%"}

Then refer to the figure in text: see @fig-hippocrates.

::: {.thought-question}
A question for the class to discuss in the moment — short, opinion-eliciting, no obvious right answer. Aim for one or two per part.

*Example:* If a competent patient refuses a life-saving transfusion on religious grounds, and their adult child begs you to override the refusal, what do you owe to each of them?
:::

## Mini-Recap

::: {.recap}
Two or three sentences synthesizing what Part A established. This bridges into Part B.
:::

# Part B — [Subtitle]

## Where We Left Off

One short paragraph reorienting students who arrive having read or watched Part A separately. Online students will see Parts A and B back to back, so don't repeat too much.

## What We're Doing in Part B

::: {.learning-outcomes}
By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

- [Outcome 1]
- [Outcome 2]
:::

## [Section 3 Title]

Body content. Build on Part A. Introduce additional [key terms]{.key-term} as needed.

::: {.thought-question}
[A second discussion prompt — ideally one that revisits a tension introduced in Part A from a new angle.]
:::

## [Section 4 Title]

Application section. Either work through a brief illustrative case in the text *or* point students to the paired case study document:

> **Read for next class:** [Case N — Title](cases/NN-slug.qmd)

## Wrap-Up

::: {.recap}
Three to four sentences summarizing the whole lecture's arc — the question, the main moves, the unresolved tension that opens onto the next lecture or the assigned case study.
:::

## Key Terms

Used in this lecture and worth being able to define on an exam:

- **[Term 1]** — short gloss
- **[Term 2]** — short gloss
- **[Term 3]** — short gloss

## Further Reading

Curated list of accessible follow-up sources. Distinct from the references below (which are works *cited* in the lecture).

- Author. *Title*. Publisher, Year.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
